Use E = 1/2 C U^2 after converting capacitance to farads when needed.
Use C = Q/U and report capacitance in microfarads.
Apply Coulomb's law F = k|q1 q2|/r^2.
At the midpoint, equal and opposite charges produce fields in the same direction, so the magnitudes add.
For series capacitors, both carry the same charge Q = C_eq V.
Use f = 1 / (2π√(LC)).
Use X_L = 2πfL and round to the nearest ohm.
Use d = v0 t + 1/2 a t^2.
Use work-energy: Fd = 1/2 m v^2.
Use Q = mcΔT.

LLM Type 2 direct fallback failed: Server error '500 Internal Server Error' for url 'https://velbertrack--exact2026-optionb-qwen25-vllm-server.modal.run/v1/chat/completions'
For more information check: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status/500
Use the ideal gas law P = nRT/V.
Use W = qU. With q in microcoulombs and U in volts, the result is in microjoules.
Use R = rho*l/S with the given consistent ohm·mm^2/m units.
The attraction toward the larger-magnitude negative charge dominates, so the net force points toward q₂.
After short-circuiting, the capacitor voltage drops to zero, so both stored charge and stored energy become zero.
Capacitance is directly proportional to the dielectric constant, so halving ε halves C.
